---
title: "Session 13 - Basics of RMarkdown"
subtitle: "This is my subtitle"
author: 
  - Member A
  - Member B
  - Member C
  - Member D
  
date: "2025-12-08"
output: pdf_document
urlcolor: blue # hyperlinks in blue
---

# Text Editing in Markdown

- This is how we add links in R Markdown:

You simply write as if it were a regular text editing document.

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a formatting syntax tool for creating HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on how to use R Markdown, see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

- We can also add hyperlinks:

This is an example of how to add hyperlinks. You can read more about R Markdown at this [link](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com).

- This is how we specify titles in RMarkdown:

# Title 1 (highest hierarchy)

Above was the highest hierarchy

## Title 2 (second-level hierarchy)

Above was the second highest hierarchy

### Title 3 (third-level hierarchy)

- We can edit our text by adding formatting:

This is an example of **bold** text. I can also **use it in full sentences or multiple words**.

We can format words in **bold**, *italics* or _underlined_

> We can include a paragraph with more indentation than a regular paragraph.

We can also include a footnote anywhere in the document.[^1]

[^1]: Footnote text. Here we can also embed links that [redirect to websites](https://cran.r-project.org/).

# Building a Numbered List

1. Item 1  
2. Item 2  
3. Test item  

# Building a Bullet List

- Item 1  
- Item 2  
- Test item 3  
- Test item 4  

# Bullet List with a Nested Numbered Sublist (4 spaces indentation)

- Item 1  
    1.1. This is my first sub-element  
- Item 2  
    1. Sub-element 1  
    2. Sub-element 2  
    3. Another sub-element  

- In R Markdown we can also add LaTeX equations:  
Inline equation example: $\alpha = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times coefficient + \lambda^2$.

Centered equation example:  
$$\alpha = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times coefficient + \frac{1}{2}$$

[This link](https://ashki23.github.io/markdown-latex.html) provides a useful guide for operators, symbols, and Greek letters.

Tambien podemos agregar las ecuaciones centradas (agregando dos simbolos de dolar) $$\alpha = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times coefficient$$ [Este enlace](https://ashki23.github.io/markdown-latex.html) es una guia util para distintos operadores, símbolos y alfabeto griego para que puedan hacer sus ecuaciones.

\pagebreak

# Tables in R Markdown

We often want to add tables from data frames into our reports. We can add a code chunk for this:


```{r}



```


```{r table 1, echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# This is an R code chunk where we specify that the code itself should not be printed,
# but the results should be printed (echo = FALSE), and that no execution messages should appear.

# We must load in Markdown ALL the packages that we are going to use inside the code chunk
library(knitr)
library(dslabs)
library(dplyr)

data(murders)

# I can process raw data directly from R Markdown, just as we have been doing in class.
murders_table <- murders %>% 
  group_by(region) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise('Average homicide rate' = weighted.mean(total, population), 
                   'Number of states' = n(), 
                   'Total population by region' = sum(population)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::rename('Region' = region)

# I want a table of murders_table

# I can simply print the data frame
murders_table

# Or I can print it using knitr's LaTeX formatting (much nicer)
kable(murders_table, caption = 'Firearm homicides in the U.S. for 2010')

```

\pagebreak

We can do exactly the same with regression!

```{r table 4, echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning= FALSE}

library(HistData)
library(fixest) # Another useful package for regressions, particularly those with fixed effects
library(huxtable)

data(GaltonFamilies)

galton_regression <- feols(childHeight ~ midparentHeight, GaltonFamilies)

# We can print the regression table
summary(galton_regression)

# Or we can use huxreg to print it in a nicer, more polished format
huxreg(galton_regression,
       statistics = c("N. obs." = "nobs", 
                      "R squared" = "r.squared", 
                      "F statistic" = "statistic",
                      "P value" = "p.value"), 
       stars = c(`*` = 0.1, `**` = 0.05, `***` = 0.01),
       # Very important: include ALL the coefficient names you want to display.
       # Otherwise, they will not appear in the table.
       coefs = c("Intercept" = "(Intercept)", 
                 "Average parent height (inches)" = "midparentHeight")) %>% 
  set_caption("Child height as a function of parent height") %>% 
  set_caption_pos("top")

# If we have more than one regression and want to specify coefficients across them,
# we can do so using huxreg

galton_regression_2 <- feols(childHeight ~ midparentHeight + childNum, GaltonFamilies)

huxreg(galton_regression, galton_regression_2,
       statistics = c("N. obs." = "nobs", 
                      "R squared" = "r.squared", 
                      "F statistic" = "statistic",
                      "P value" = "p.value"), 
       stars = c(`*` = 0.1, `**` = 0.05, `***` = 0.01),
       # Very important: include ALL the coefficient names you want to display.
       # Otherwise, they will not appear in the table.
       coefs = c("Intercept" = "(Intercept)", 
                 "Average parent height (inches)" = "midparentHeight",
                 "Number of children in household" = "childNum")) %>% 
  set_caption("Child height as a function of parent height") %>% 
  set_caption_pos("top")


```

\pagebreak

# Add graphs into R Markdown

Just as we embedded tables, we can embed plots:

```{r tabla 2, echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}

library(ggplot2)
data(murders)

p <- murders %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = population/10^6, y = total, label = abb))


p + geom_point(size = 3) +  
  geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  scale_y_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + 
  ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
  ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))


```

\pagebreak

# Referencias en R Markdown

With what we've learned so far, we can build a simple reference section at the end of our reports. But there is a more powerful option:

There are reference managers like [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/) allow us to store references online (or via DOI). This is extremely helpful for academic papers or theses, because Zotero can automatically generate a bibliography section. Zotero is compatible with R Markdown, MS Word, and Google Docs.

Esto resulta particularmente útil para trabajos universitarios o trabajos de grado, debido a que haciendo uso de Zotero correctamente, nos permite generar de forma automática la sección de referencias al final de nuestro informe o trabajo. Zotero es compatible con R Markdown, así como es compatible con Microsoft Word y Google Docs.

**I recommend exploring Zotero for adding citations (especially thesis work).**

Here is also a useful [a link](https://bookdown.org/gboccardo/manual-ED-UCH/introduccion-al-uso-de-rmarkdown-para-la-compilacion-de-resultados-de-rstudio-en-diferentes-formatos.html) explaining how to use Zotero with R Markdown through LaTeX bibliography formatting.
